text: Laparoscopic duodenal jejunostomy for SMA syndrome presented by the VCU Minimally Invasive Surgery Center. The patient is a fifty-eight year old female who presented with nausea, vomiting, and vague abdominal discomfort after significant weight loss. She had recently lost her job and had chronic anxiety disorder contributing to her weight loss.
Her physical exam is unremarkable. She is slim and had a non-tender abdomen. Her abdomen is flat and has no surgical scars.
The CT scan image shows a markedly dilated stomach and a narrow duodenum at the aero-mesenteric angle, confirming the findings from the EGD.

Her diagnosis is superior mesenteric artery syndrome. As the patient loses weight, there is a loss of the mesenteric fat pack. The outer mesenteric angle is sharper and results in an occluded duodenum.

The surgeon prepares to bypass the duodenal obstruction by performing a duodenal jejunostomy. Abdominal insufflation is achieved using a varus needle inserted into the left upper quadrant, with ports placed in a specific configuration for the procedure.

The surgeon begins the procedure by identifying the ligament of Treitz. The transverse colon is retracted cephalad to expose the area. The peritoneum overlying the visibly dilated duodenum is identified and cleared, located to the right of the superior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon brings the jejunum approximately thirty centimeters from the ligament of Treitz into apposition with the duodenum. Stay sutures using zero Surgidac suture are placed around the intended site of anastomosis, with two sutures around the site and a third distally to ensure a straight course for the stapler.

The surgeon creates an anterotomy on the duodenal side using an ultrasonic scalpel, ensuring intraluminal access with a prestige grasper. An anterotomy is also created on the jejunal side. An endo-GIA stapler with a 2.5 mm white load cartridge is introduced into the anterotomy and fired.

The surgeon encloses the common aneurotomy using a 3-0 polysorb suture, carefully evaluating the integrity of both the suture line and the staple line. The trocars are then removed from the surgical field.

Postoperatively, the patient underwent a contrast study demonstrating no leak. Her diet is advanced and she is discharged home. On follow-up, she has no obstructive symptoms, nor does she have any abdominal pain. Her only complaint is weight gain.
